annotations.label: Function (informal)

anchor.snippet: a function from a set X to a set Y assigns to each element of X exactly one element of Y

annotations.note: Functions are the built-in type `α → β` in Lean's type theory; there is no Mathlib `def` because the notion is primitive.

annotations.label: Graph of a function

anchor.snippet: a function is uniquely represented by the set of all pairs

annotations.note: `Function.graph f = {(a, b) | f a = b}` matches the graph definition.

annotations.label: Function, domain and codomain

anchor.snippet: A function f from a set X to a set Y is an assignment of one element of Y to each element of X

annotations.note: Lean's primitive arrow type `α → β` provides domain `α` and codomain `β` directly.

annotations.label: Image of a function (range)

anchor.snippet: The image of a function , sometimes called its range , is the set of the images of all elements in the domain

annotations.note: `Set.range f` is the set of images of all elements of the domain.

annotations.label: Partial function (informal)

anchor.snippet: Such a function is then called a partial function

annotations.note: `PFun α β` (notation `α →. β`) is the type of partial functions defined as `α → Part β`.

annotations.label: Function on a set S

anchor.snippet: A function f on a set S means a function from the domain S , without specifying a codomain

annotations.note: Lean's type system always requires both a domain and codomain, so this informal usage is not separately formalized.

annotations.label: Square function

anchor.snippet: the square function is the function

annotations.note: The square function `x ↦ x^2` is `sq` / `· ^ 2` in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Binary relation

anchor.snippet: a binary relation between two sets X and Y is a subset of the set of all ordered pairs

annotations.note: `Rel α β := α → β → Prop` formalizes a binary relation between `α` and `β`.

annotations.label: Cartesian product of two sets

anchor.snippet: The set of all these pairs is called the Cartesian product of X and Y and denoted

annotations.note: `Set.prod s t = {p | p.1 ∈ s ∧ p.2 ∈ t}` realizes the binary Cartesian product; on types it is `Prod`.

annotations.label: Function as functional relation (set-theoretic)

anchor.snippet: A function with domain X and codomain Y is a binary relation R between X and Y that satisfies the two following conditions

annotations.note: `SetRel.exists_graph_eq_iff` characterizes which relations come from functions (`∀ a, ∃! b, a ~[R] b`), giving the set-theoretic functional-relation criterion.

annotations.label: Functional relation

anchor.snippet: A relation satisfying these conditions is called a functional relation

annotations.note: The condition `∀ a, ∃! b, a ~[R] b` in `SetRel.exists_graph_eq_iff` captures the functional-relation predicate.

annotations.label: Partial function (formal)

anchor.snippet: Partial functions are defined similarly to ordinary functions, with the "total" condition removed

annotations.note: `PFun α β` is exactly a partial function from `α` to `β`.

annotations.label: Domain of definition; total function

anchor.snippet: is called the domain of definition of the function

annotations.note: `PFun.Dom` gives the domain of definition of a partial function.

annotations.label: Real function (real-valued function of a real variable)

anchor.snippet: a real-valued function of a real variable or real function is a partial function from the set

annotations.note: Mathlib uses plain `ℝ → ℝ` for total real functions; a partial real function would be `PFun ℝ ℝ`, but there is no named alias.

annotations.label: Function of a complex variable

anchor.snippet: a function of a complex variable is generally a partial function whose domain of definition is a subset of the complex numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib uses `ℂ → ℂ` (or restrictions to opens) without a named partial-function alias.

annotations.label: Riemann zeta function and Riemann hypothesis

anchor.snippet: the multiplicative inverse of the Riemann zeta function

annotations.note: `riemannZeta : ℂ → ℂ` is the Riemann zeta function; the Riemann hypothesis itself is not yet a theorem in Mathlib.

annotations.label: General recursive function

anchor.snippet: a general recursive function is a partial function from the integers to the integers whose values can be computed by an algorithm

annotations.note: `Nat.Partrec : (ℕ →. ℕ) → Prop` is the inductive definition of partial recursive (general recursive) functions.

annotations.label: Undecidability of recursive-function membership (Halting problem)

anchor.snippet: A fundamental theorem of computability theory is that there cannot exist an algorithm that takes an arbitrary general recursive function as input

annotations.note: `ComputablePred.halting_problem` proves `¬ComputablePred fun c => (c.eval n).Dom`, the undecidability of the halting predicate.

annotations.label: Multivariate function

anchor.snippet: A multivariate function , multivariable function , or function of several variables is a function that depends on several arguments

annotations.note: Multivariate functions are simply curried/uncurried Lean functions; `Function.uncurry`/`Function.curry` (in core/Mathlib) provide the conversion.

annotations.label: Function of n variables

anchor.snippet: a function of n variables is a function whose domain is a set of n -tuples

annotations.note: Functions of n variables are expressed in Lean as `(Fin n → α) → β` or as iterated arrows; not a separate Mathlib `def`.

annotations.label: Multiplication of integers as bivariate function

anchor.snippet: multiplication of integers is a function of two variables, or bivariate function

annotations.note: Integer multiplication is realized as a binary function via `Int.mul` (and the `Mul ℤ` instance).

annotations.label: Cartesian product of n sets

anchor.snippet: is called the Cartesian product of

annotations.note: `Set.prod` provides binary Cartesian product of sets; `Set.pi` provides the n-ary version.

annotations.label: Functional notation

anchor.snippet: The functional notation requires that a name is given to the function

annotations.note: Functional notation is a metamathematical convention, not a Mathlib definition.

annotations.label: Arrow notation

anchor.snippet: Arrow notation defines the rule of a function inline, without requiring a name to be given to the function

annotations.note: Lean's `fun x => ...` (lambda) syntax realizes arrow/anonymous-function notation.

annotations.label: Index notation; sequence

anchor.snippet: Such a function is called a sequence

annotations.note: Sequences in Lean/Mathlib are simply functions `ℕ → α`; no named `def` because the type itself suffices.

annotations.label: Placeholder notation

anchor.snippet: the symbol x does not represent any value; it is simply a placeholder

annotations.note: Placeholder notation is a notational convention with no Mathlib definition.

annotations.label: Function of two variables: sum of squares

anchor.section: Functions of more than one variable
anchor.snippet: a function f can be defined as mapping any pair of real numbers

annotations.note: Specific illustrative example; not a named declaration in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Map / mapping

anchor.snippet: A function may also be called a map or a mapping

annotations.note: Terminological remark; no separate Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Function defined by listing values on a finite set

anchor.snippet: On a finite set a function may be defined by listing the elements of the codomain that are associated to the elements of the domain

annotations.note: `Function.update` plus pattern-matching on finite domains realize functions specified by listing values.

annotations.label: Quadratic function

anchor.snippet: A quadratic function is a function that may be written

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Polynomial` and `QuadraticForm` but no general `def QuadraticFunction` for ax²+bx+c on a single variable.

annotations.label: Polynomial function

anchor.snippet: a polynomial function is a function that can be defined by a formula involving only additions, subtractions, multiplications, and exponentiation to nonnegative integer powers

annotations.note: `Polynomial R` represents polynomials and `Polynomial.eval` gives the associated polynomial function.

annotations.label: Rational function

anchor.snippet: A rational function is the same, with divisions also allowed

annotations.note: `RatFunc K` is Mathlib's formalization of the field of rational functions over `K`.

annotations.label: Algebraic function

anchor.snippet: An algebraic function is the same, with n th roots and roots of polynomials also allowed

annotations.note: No general `def AlgebraicFunction` in Mathlib; only related notions like `IsAlgebraic` for elements of an extension.

annotations.label: Elementary function

anchor.snippet: An elementary function [ note 4 ] is the same, with logarithms and exponential functions allowed

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formal definition of the class of elementary functions.

annotations.label: Bijective function and inverse function

anchor.snippet: is bijective , if for every y in Y , there is one and only one element x in X such that y = f ( x )

annotations.note: `Function.Bijective f := Injective f ∧ Surjective f`, with `Equiv.ofBijective` building the inverse function.

annotations.label: Natural logarithm as bijection; exponential as inverse

anchor.snippet: the natural logarithm is a bijective function from the positive real numbers to the real numbers

annotations.note: `Real.log_injOn_pos` and `Real.exp_log` together give that `Real.log` is a bijection `(0, ∞) → ℝ` with inverse `Real.exp`.

annotations.label: Arccosine as inverse of restricted cosine

anchor.snippet: its inverse function, called arccosine

annotations.note: `Real.arccos : ℝ → ℝ` is the inverse cosine, with `Real.cos_arccos` proving the inversion on `[-1, 1]`.

annotations.label: Implicit function (from a relation)

anchor.snippet: called an implicit function , because it is implicitly defined by the relation R

annotations.note: `HasStrictFDerivAt.implicitFunction` realizes implicit functions in the analytic setting; there is no general Mathlib `def` for implicit functions from arbitrary relations.

annotations.label: Implicit functions defined by the unit circle

anchor.snippet: For example, the equation of the unit circle

annotations.note: Specific illustrative example; not a named Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Bring radical as an implicit function

anchor.snippet: called the Bring radical

annotations.note: The Bring radical is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Implicit function theorem

anchor.snippet: The implicit function theorem provides mild differentiability conditions for existence and uniqueness of an implicit function in the neighborhood of a point

annotations.note: `HasStrictFDerivAt.implicitFunction` provides the implicit function theorem in Banach spaces.

annotations.label: Function defined as an antiderivative

anchor.snippet: Many functions can be defined as the antiderivative of another function

annotations.note: Antiderivatives are not packaged as a named operator, but `intervalIntegral` and `FTC` lemmas realize the antiderivative construction.

annotations.label: Exponential function as solution of a differential equation

anchor.snippet: the exponential function , which can be defined as the unique function that is equal to its derivative and takes the value 1 for x = 0

annotations.note: `Real.exp` is defined via its power series (not as ODE solution), but its derivative property `Real.hasDerivAt_exp` is proved.

annotations.label: Function defined by a power series

anchor.snippet: Power series can be used to define functions on the domain in which they converge

annotations.note: `HasFPowerSeriesAt f p x` and `HasFPowerSeriesOnBall` express that `f` is given by a power series on a neighborhood.

annotations.label: Definition by recurrence

anchor.snippet: Functions whose domain are the nonnegative integers, known as sequences , are sometimes defined by recurrence relations

annotations.note: Lean's built-in `Nat.rec`/`Nat.recOn` and `def` by pattern matching directly support recurrence-defined functions.

annotations.label: Factorial function by recurrence

anchor.snippet: The factorial function on the nonnegative integers

annotations.note: `Nat.factorial` is defined by primitive recursion: `0! = 1`, `(n+1)! = (n+1) * n!`.

annotations.label: Graph of a function (formal)

anchor.snippet: its graph is, formally, the set

annotations.note: `Function.graph f := {(a, b) | f a = b}` is exactly the formal set-of-pairs definition.

annotations.label: Graph of the square function: parabola / Fermat's spiral

anchor.snippet: the graph of the square function

annotations.note: Specific illustrative example; no Mathlib declaration for the parabola/Fermat-spiral pictures.

annotations.label: Function represented by a table of values

anchor.snippet: A function can be represented as a table of values

annotations.note: Tabular representation is a presentational device, not a Mathlib definition.

annotations.label: Bar chart representation of a function

anchor.snippet: A bar chart can represent a function whose domain is a finite set, the natural numbers , or the integers

annotations.note: Bar charts are graphical, not a Mathlib formalization target.

annotations.label: Empty function

anchor.snippet: there is a unique function, called the empty function , or empty map , from the empty set to X

annotations.note: `isEmptyElim` produces a unique function out of any empty type; `isEmpty_fun` characterizes when `α → β` is empty.

annotations.label: Canonical surjection onto image

anchor.snippet: the canonical surjection of f onto its image

annotations.note: `Set.rangeFactorization f : α → range f` is the canonical surjection onto the image; `coe_comp_rangeFactorization` shows it factors `f`.

annotations.label: Inclusion map

anchor.snippet: the inclusion map of A into X is the injective (see below) function that maps every element of A to itself

annotations.note: `Set.inclusion (h : s ⊆ t) : s → t := fun x ↦ ⟨x, h x.prop⟩` is exactly the inclusion map.

annotations.label: Identity function

anchor.snippet: The identity function on a set X , often denoted by id X , is the inclusion of X into itself

annotations.note: `id : α → α` is the Lean core identity function used throughout Mathlib.

annotations.label: Function composition

anchor.snippet: such that the domain of g is the codomain of f , their composition is the function

annotations.note: `Function.comp` (notation `∘`) is Lean's primitive composition `(f ∘ g) x = f (g x)`.

annotations.label: Non-commutativity of composition

anchor.snippet: the composition is not necessarily commutative

annotations.note: Non-commutativity is not stated as a theorem in Mathlib; only specific counterexamples appear.

annotations.label: Associativity of function composition

anchor.snippet: The function composition is associative in the sense that

annotations.note: `Function.comp_assoc` proves `(f ∘ g) ∘ h = f ∘ g ∘ h`.

annotations.label: Identity functions are left/right identities

anchor.snippet: are respectively a right identity and a left identity for functions from X to Y

annotations.note: `Function.id_comp` and `Function.comp_id` express that `id` is a two-sided identity for composition.

annotations.label: Image of a subset under a function

anchor.snippet: the image of A under f , denoted f ( A ) , is the subset of the codomain Y consisting of all images of elements of A

annotations.note: `Set.image f s = {f a | a ∈ s}`, with notation `f '' s`.

annotations.label: Preimage of an element

anchor.snippet: the inverse image or preimage under f of an element y of the codomain Y is the set of all elements of the domain X whose images under f equal y

annotations.note: The preimage of a single element is `f ⁻¹' {y}`, a special case of `Set.preimage`.

annotations.label: Preimage of a subset

anchor.snippet: the preimage of a subset B of the codomain Y is the set of the preimages of the elements of B

annotations.note: `Set.preimage f s = {x | f x ∈ s}`, notation `f ⁻¹' s`.

annotations.label: Fiber of a point

anchor.snippet: is sometimes called, in some contexts, the fiber of y under f

annotations.note: The fiber of `y` under `f` is `f ⁻¹' {y}`, a special case of `Set.preimage`.

annotations.label: Injective function

anchor.snippet: The function f is injective (or one-to-one , or is an injection ) if f ( a ) ≠ f ( b ) for every two different elements a and b of X

annotations.note: `Function.Injective f := ∀ ⦃a b⦄, f a = f b → a = b` is in Lean core and used throughout Mathlib.

annotations.label: Injective iff has a left inverse

anchor.snippet: f is injective if and only if there exists a function

annotations.note: `Function.injective_iff_hasLeftInverse` proves `Injective f ↔ HasLeftInverse f`.

annotations.label: Surjective function

anchor.snippet: The function f is surjective (or onto , or is a surjection ) if its range

annotations.note: `Function.Surjective f := ∀ b, ∃ a, f a = b` is in Lean core.

annotations.label: Surjective iff has a right inverse (uses choice)

anchor.snippet: f is surjective if and only if there exists a function

annotations.note: `Function.surjective_iff_hasRightInverse` proves `Surjective f ↔ HasRightInverse f`, using classical choice via `surjInv`.

annotations.label: Bijective function

anchor.snippet: The function f is bijective (or is a bijection or a one-to-one correspondence ) if it is both injective and surjective

annotations.note: `Function.Bijective f := Injective f ∧ Surjective f`.

annotations.label: Bijective iff admits an inverse function

anchor.snippet: The function f is bijective if and only if it admits an inverse function

annotations.note: `Function.bijective_iff_has_inverse` proves that bijective is equivalent to having a two-sided inverse.

annotations.label: Canonical factorization of a function

anchor.snippet: may be factorized as the composition

annotations.note: `Set.rangeFactorization` and `coe_comp_rangeFactorization` give the surjection-injection factorization through the image, but the full quotient/image/inclusion three-factor decomposition is not packaged as a single theorem.

annotations.label: Restriction of a function

anchor.snippet: then the restriction of

annotations.note: `Set.restrict s f : ↥s → _` restricts `f` to a subset `s`.

annotations.label: Extension of a function

anchor.snippet: An extension of a function f is a function g such that f is a restriction of g

annotations.note: `Function.extend f g g₀` extends `g` along an injection `f` (with default `g₀`).

annotations.label: Analytic continuation

anchor.snippet: A typical use of this concept is the process of analytic continuation , that allows extending functions whose domain is a small part of the complex plane to functions whose domain is almost the whole complex plane

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the uniqueness of analytic continuation (identity theorem) on preconnected sets, the principal ingredient enabling extension of analytic functions.

annotations.label: Homography of the real line

anchor.snippet: A homography is a function

annotations.note: No `def Homography` for the real-line Möbius transformation in Mathlib; only projective-geometry homographies in different contexts.

annotations.label: Real function (calculus section)

anchor.snippet: A real function is a real-valued function of a real variable , that is, a function whose codomain is the field of real numbers and whose domain is a set of real numbers that contains an interval

annotations.note: A real function is just `ℝ → ℝ` (or `s → ℝ` for `s : Set ℝ`); no separate Mathlib alias.

annotations.label: Pointwise operations on real functions

anchor.snippet: Functions enjoy pointwise operations

annotations.note: `Pi.instAdd`/`Pi.instMul`/etc. equip function spaces with pointwise algebraic operations.

annotations.label: Polynomial / rational real functions

anchor.snippet: The polynomial functions are defined by polynomials , and their domain is the whole set of real numbers

annotations.note: `Polynomial ℝ` and `RatFunc ℝ` provide polynomial and rational real functions via `eval`.

annotations.label: Antiderivative; the natural logarithm

anchor.snippet: An antiderivative of a continuous real function is a real function that has the original function as a derivative

annotations.note: `Real.log` is defined in Mathlib (via `Complex.log`), with antiderivative property `Real.deriv_log` available.

annotations.label: Monotonic real function

anchor.snippet: A real function f is monotonic in an interval if the sign of

annotations.note: `Monotone`, `StrictMono`, `MonotoneOn`, `StrictMonoOn` formalize various notions of monotonicity.

annotations.label: Monotonic function has an inverse

anchor.snippet: If a real function f is monotonic in an interval I , it has an inverse function

annotations.note: `StrictMonoOn.orderIso` builds an order isomorphism between a set and its image for a strictly monotone function on that set.

annotations.label: Sine and cosine as solutions of the linear ODE y'' = -y

anchor.snippet: the sine and the cosine functions are the solutions of the linear differential equation

annotations.note: `Real.sin`/`Real.cos` are defined via complex exponentials; their second-derivative identities (`Real.cos_neg_sin`-style) realize the ODE `y'' = -y`.

annotations.label: Vector-valued function

anchor.snippet: the function is said to be a vector-valued function

annotations.note: Vector-valued functions are functions into a vector space (e.g. `α → E` with `Module 𝕜 E`); not a named alias.

annotations.label: Vector field

anchor.snippet: These vector-valued functions are given the name vector fields

annotations.note: Smooth vector fields on manifolds are realized as smooth sections of the tangent bundle; Mathlib also has `Analysis/Calculus/VectorField.lean` for the Euclidean setting.

annotations.label: Function space

anchor.snippet: a function space is a set of scalar-valued or vector-valued functions , which share a specific property and form a topological vector space

annotations.note: Mathlib has many specific function spaces (`ContinuousMap`, `Lp`, `BoundedContinuousFunction`, `Schwartz`, …) but no overarching `def FunctionSpace`.

annotations.label: Square root as a multi-valued function

anchor.snippet: in defining the square root as the inverse function of the square function

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Real.sqrt`/`Complex.sqrt` are principal-branch single-valued; the multi-valued square root is not formalized.

annotations.label: Multi-valued function; branch cut; principal value; monodromy

anchor.snippet: One may define a function that is not continuous along some curve, called a branch cut

annotations.note: Multi-valued functions, branch cuts, principal values, and monodromy are not formalized as a general framework in Mathlib (only specific monodromy notions appear in homotopy lifting).

annotations.label: Replacement axiom (NBG set theory)

anchor.snippet: This theory includes the replacement axiom , which may be stated as: If X is a set and F is a function, then F [ X ] is a set

annotations.note: Lean/Mathlib is founded on type theory (CIC), not NBG; the replacement axiom is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: General recursive functions (operators)

anchor.snippet: General recursive functions are partial functions from integers to integers that can be defined from

annotations.note: `Nat.Partrec` inductively defines partial recursive functions via zero, successor, projection, pairing, composition, primitive recursion, and `rfind`.

annotations.label: Equivalence of models of computation; Church–Turing thesis

anchor.snippet: The fundamental theorem of computability theory is that these three models of computation define the same set of computable functions

annotations.note: `Turing.PartrecToTM2.tr_eval` formalizes that a TM2 machine simulates partial-recursive evaluation, but a comprehensive Church-Turing equivalence covering all standard models is not packaged.

annotations.label: Lambda calculus

anchor.snippet: Lambda calculus is a theory that defines computable functions without using set theory

annotations.note: The untyped lambda calculus is not formalized as a standalone theory in Mathlib (though Lean itself is based on a typed λ-calculus).
